comments.title: BRH
comments.content:
Dear Archdruid Emeritus,

Blessings to you and to all!

My one year anniversary of doing GD magic is approaching. I remember it was around August last year when I began doing the LBRP with a sense of purpose after a difficult period in my life. This was then followed shortly by the MP. Eventually, I added the Four Solar Adorations and a daily tarot contemplation ritual. My practice has been very consistent for the most part and I’ve seen the improvements in visualizations, sense perception and divination skills. My question is about the next stage of magical training. Based on the books and videos I’ve been reading and watching, the next step would be to do the BRH. I know some people do it earlier but I really want at least a year to pass before attempting this ritual. If I add the BRH, so that my daily ritual looks like this: LBRP, BRH, MP (Advanced with Circulation); would this be a balanced and sustainable ritual to do for the foreseeable future? What are some effects that one can expect from this addition?

Many thanks again for hosting this forum.

Jean Luc

replies.title: Re: BRH
replies.content:
I don't recommend the hexagram ritual as part of daily practice. Banishing the influence of one planet over and over again is not a good idea! What I recommend instead is that you start doing a basic temple opening and closing ritual -- the Watchtower Ritual is one example, the Neophyte formula opening and closing in my book Circles of Power is another. Choose one and do it once a week.

Once you can do it from memory, add in the invoking and banishing hexagram rituals as follows: opening ritual (includes LBRP and LBRH), invoking BRH of a planet, meditation on that planet, banishing BRH of that planet, closing ritual (includes LBRP and LBRH). Work with a different planetary energy each week, starting with the Moon and working up to Saturn, then repeat. This is a very effective way of self-initiation into magical energies and can take you very far indeed.

replies.title: Re: BRH
replies.content:
Thank you, JMG!

I'll make sure to get a copy of Circles of Power.


Jean Luc

replies.title: Re: BRH
replies.content:
I had wondered about adding opening and closing rituals myself, after several years of doing GD magic. But I live with someone who is hyper-sensitive to smells, candles and incense, and is often made ill by them. So there's no chance of doing many of these rituals. Would a completely virtual, visualised, ritual, be better than nothing? And would it enable me to go on to the invoking and banishing rituals as you suggest in the second paragraph?
Thank you.

replies.title: Re: BRH
replies.content: You can do without the candles, incense et al. Get battery-powered electric candles for your altar, and in place of the incense, get a smaller electric candle, put it on a red or flame-colored dish or bowl, and use that as your symbol of fire. I've used battery candles in hotel settings where flame was forbidden and have had good results with them, so you should be fine.

replies.title: Re: BRH
replies.content:
Hi, JMG- not the OP, but he asked a very similar question to the one I was planning to ask this week (about the introduction of Hexagram Rituals into regular practice), and I'm hoping to follow up:

You'd suggested a pretty similar curriculum to the OP above to me on a previous Magic Monday:
"Then learn the Opening and Closing Rituals in Chapter 8. Do those weekly as well. Then, once or twice a week, do a working where you open a temple, invoke one of the elements using the Greater Ritual of the Pentagram, meditate on the element, banish the element and close. Do this with all four elements, say, four times each. Then do the same thing with the planetary energies, using the Hexagram rituals. Then move on to basic rituals of practical magic. All this time, maintain your basic practices from LRM. That sequence of practices will take you very far indeed."

1. I'm just reaching a point where I can do the basic forms of opening and closing a temple given in Circles of Power from memory (I've been doing them weekly for a couple of months now). When invoking and meditating on the elements using the working you suggested above, would you suggest using the basic form of opening and closing, or the complete form?

2. I tried going through the complete forms of opening and closing a temple in Circles of Power this weekend, and did a pretty fumbling job of it since I'd never practiced the Hexagram rituals before. While I see that it's ill-advised to work these into daily practice, it was pretty evident that I could use some practice just gaining fluency with the basic forms of the rituals before integrating them into anything more complex. Do you have any thoughts on how to do this in a way that would be balanced, safe, and hopefully useful, or would it be best to wait until I'm ready to invoke and meditate on the planetary energies, and expect a bunch of clumsy page-turning the first few times? FWIW, I've also been doing a roughly weekly practice of the LBRP-GIRP-Greater Middle Pillar Exercise-LBRP, on a previous suggestion you once made, and which I've found very helpful. Possible to work some Hexagram practice into this sequence?

replies.title: Re: BRH
replies.content:
1) Use the basic form until you feel ready to add in the rest of it, then do the complete form.

2) Clumsy page turning is fine. We all go through that stage.

comments.title: Lord (of Fallhall)'s Prayer and is Pluto Qlippothic?
comments.content:
Good Evening!

To those who celebrate Lughnasadh (or equivalent "cross-quarter day") by the calendar, I wish you a happy and blessed day. For those who prefer to celebrate by the halfway-point between the solstice and equinox, I'll have happy wishes for you next week. For me, this marks one year of celebrating the 8-point Wheel of the Year and one year of improved bread baking.

To Share: This started as mostly a joke, but as I worked on it, it became. . . less so. I wrote a prayer to Woden that mostly follows the cadence of the "Our Father"/"Lord's Prayer" in English. I'm new to this whole poetry thing, but I figure showing things in public is part of the process of getting better: https://jprussell.dreamwidth.org/691.html

To Ask: Forgive me if this is answered in Twilight of Pluto, but I haven't read that due to my pledge this year to read books by dead authors. I was listening to your recent appearance on the Hermetic Astrology podcast, and you mentioned how Pluto was likely just the closest large body in the Kuiper belt, made of comet-stuff. This put me in mind of Dion Fortune's description of comets in The Cosmic Doctrine as basically the tools for gathering those souls that have rejected the round of evolution represented by the current version of our solar system, and linking them with the Qlippoth, if I remember rightly. So, my question is: do you think Pluto is/was a part of this same outsider contingent?

Thanks very much for your time, thought, and making this space such a worthwhile forum.

Cheers,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Lord (of Fallhall)'s Prayer and is Pluto Qlippothic?
replies.content:
And a happy Lughnasadh to you and yours.

As for Pluto, hmm! That's a supposition worth exploring. Certainly the Plutonian current seems to have led a lot of people to self-destructive extremes. The one difference between Pluto and the comets Fortune discusses is that Pluto never enters the inner solar system -- his orbit dips inside that of Neptune, though, and that may count.

replies.title: Re: Lord (of Fallhall)'s Prayer and is Pluto Qlippothic?
replies.content:
Not JMG.

I would suggest that Pluto's role in guiding souls through the initiation of the nadir is an evolutionary purpose, and not associated with the Qlippoth. It's as if Pluto was an appointed edge lord for a time, but still of the solar system (albeit aware of what is beyond).

comments.title: Fixed Star Talismans
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

Thanks for hosting these weekly forums. I have a few questions about fixed star talismans:

1) You've mentioned in previous answers that a planetary talisman's effects can differ based on that planet's condition in the owner's natal chart, and that if the planet is debilitated or harshly aspected, the effects tend to be negative. Does the same principle apply to fixed star talismans? For example, suppose a magician creates a Regulus talisman, but has Mars squaring Regulus in her natal chart. Would the effects of the talisman tend to be a negative manifestation of Regulus (grandiosity and overreaching instead of dignified, capable leadership)?

2) If the answer to 1) is yes, does it matter whether the planet in the natal chart is applying or separating from the aspect?

3) Finally, a question about protective talismans. What are the karmic consequences of a fixed star talisman that's purely defensive in nature (blocks or disposes of anything harmful coming your way, but doesn't seek any specific effect on whoever's sending it), versus one that actively reflects harmful energy back to the sender? I’ve seen some descriptions of Algol talismans that seem to be the latter - just wondering what kind of karmic load one would take on by using something like that.

Thanks again for answering these questions.

replies.title: Re: Fixed Star Talismans
replies.content:
1) I don't know anything in the old literature that discusses that, but if you've got a bad aspect to a fixed star, I'd be very cautious about doing a talisman of that star. There are always other options. One thing to keep in mind, though, is that aspects to stars have very narrow orbs.

2) An applying aspect would probably be worse but I'd be cautious either way.

3) The purely defensive talisman racks up good karma for you. You're removing malefic energy from the world, so there'll be less malefic energy in your present and future life. Reflecting back hostile magic on the sender has a very specific karmic effect: you get whatever karma you earn more quickly and more forcefully. If you go out of your way to generate lots of positive karma and none of the other kind, that's fine, but how many of us can manage that? Myself, I recommend the wholly defensive approach; if you get good at that, you'll find that it's possible to use hostile magical energy as a source of power for your own workings, and that can be really entertaining.

replies.title: Re: Fixed Star Talismans
replies.content:
Hi JMG,

This sparks a question: I purchased a strictly protective moon talisman from Christopher Warnock several years ago, and do the little consecration ceremony on the first Monday after the new moon, at moon hour, monthly. (It isn’t a requirement; I just enjoy doing it.)

Even though I did not create this talisman, is my purchasing, consecrating, and wearing of it actually generating good karma?

If yes, my mind is blown on Magic Monday. It sure won’t be the first time.

Thanks as always,
OtterGirl

replies.title: Re: Fixed Star Talismans
replies.content: If it's soaking up nasty magic and not allowing it to take effect, yes, though most of that will be paid back to you by the fact that you won't suffer from the nasty magic. If anyone else is protected by it, though, you get karma for that.

replies.title: Re: Fixed Star Talismans
replies.content:
Thank you! My mind is blown. May I ask a follow up question?

The protective aspect of this purchased moon talisman is directed specifically toward "enemies and the government". While I'm unaware of any enemies per se, the government does indeed seem to be a generic candidate for that slot, given the past few years of crazy in our fine land.

None of us are an island. If I receive protection from "the government" would those I closely associate with also receive protection? Good karma received or not, I'd like to think that they, too, somehow benefit from me wearing my talisman.

Thanks again!
OtterGirl

replies.title: Re: Fixed Star Talismans
replies.content: It depends entirely on the intention for which the talisman was consecrated. If your intention was that you would be protected, it will protect you. If you included anyone else explicitly in the intention, then they're included. Talismans do exactly what they're told to do, which is why it's very important to think through the intention before the talisman is consecrated.

replies.title: Re: Fixed Star Talismans
replies.content: What does one do with a bronze talisman that hasn't worked out? Is it okay to bury it, or to give it to someone that doesn't have any bad aspects to the fixed star?

replies.title: Re: Fixed Star Talismans
replies.content: If you made it, you should deconsecrate it. If you didn't make it, it should be returned to the maker for deconsecration.

You have mentioned that raising your level of consciousness and improving your karma are key ways to help move oneself towards exiting the cycle of human incarnations and entering the next level of being. replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) "Good" and "bad" are value judgments, of course, and so the much-maligned Golden Rule is a useful guide -- good karma is generated by the kind of thing you'd want other people to do to you, bad karma is the reverse.

2) It really depends on circumstances and personal needs. If you have a lot of impulsive behaviors, a more general phrasing might be more useful.

3) I'm kind of baffled by this, as the first half of the affirmation isn't especially cryptic. It proposes that life is a continuum throughout the universe -- a very widely held concept, btw -- and affirms the identity of one's individual life with that continuum.

4) I haven't decided yet.

replies.title: Re: Apollo
replies.content:
On 2), I'm not aware of a "novelization" approach to Greek mythology, where everything is worked out to be consistent the way Gaiman did with Norse Mythology, but I'd second Edith Hamilton's book for a good, solid collection of mythology. I've heard very good things about Stephen Fry's Mythos from a fellow former classics student, but I haven't checked it out yet. Various children's collections have the advantage of art, sometimes quite lovely, depending on your taste.

Also, if you are open to comic books, a couple of suggestions: 1) Eric Shanower has a series called Age of Bronze, which is ambitious, but not completed yet. Shanower's mission is to tell the entire span of stories about the Trojan war, from Homer to Shakespeare, but make them all fit together and depict them with our most up-to-date understanding of Bronze Age archaeology. The Gods and Goddesses are handled by the humans that interact with them talking about their experiences (sometimes illustrated), which leaves it ambiguous as to whether the work is treating them as "real".

2) Not exactly accurate in every respect, but if you appreciate Gaiman's touch with mythical themes, the Furies of Greek Myth play a big role in the latter part of the Sandman series, and the whole series deals with questions of myth, archetype, and so forth.

I hope some of this helps!
Jeff

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) You're confusing magic with religion, and with dogmatic religion at that. Magic does not claim to define the nature of divinity; it simply teaches you how to work with the energies that descend from above the human level, and do things with them. 2) It hasn't been published. I wrote a chapter on the subject for an anthology for a publisher, and then later on offered the publisher a book on the subject; they turned it down, and because of the details of the contrac for the chapter, I need to wait a few more years before I can write it and place it with another publisher.

Dear JMG,
A question about affirmations if you'd be so kind.
I've suffered from an irregular heartbeat for many years now, for which I have to take medicines. I have tried, and am trying, all sorts of approaches, both medical and non-medical: it's clear the irregularity has become chronic, but is also exacerbated by moments of stress and tension, even quite mild ones.
I have generally had poor results with affirmations in the past, because my slippery unconscious mind finds ways round them. If I start affirming "my heart beats regularly" for example, it will reply "no it doesn't" and manufacture some trivial episode of stress which will send the heart into arrhythmia.

So my question is whether a more subtle form of affirmation might help. I wondered about "every day my heartbeat becomes a little more regular," or alternatively "I create the conditions for an increasingly regular heartbeat." I don't think a frontal assault is going to work, in other words.
What would your advice be?
Thanks

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: My advice is to experiment and find what works for you. If those subtler methods function, by all means. One thing to consider, however, is whether journaling might be helpful; it sounds to me, given what you've said about your mind's reaction to affirmations, that some part of you doesn't want to get well. Figuring out why that is, and seeing if you can find a less self-defeating way to deal with whatever the issues are, might be worth trying.

comments.title: Loki's messages going around in the Neo-Pagan blogsphere: Mania
comments.content:
Recently J. Beckett reported on a Seidr that he attended with messages from Loki. Since then, these messages have been burning up the Neo-Pagan blogsphere.

1. Joy! There must be joy and we must make sure in this time that is dark and growing darker with war and disease and death and all the rest, joy or else my son [Fenris] will eat the world.

2. Loki says it’s about to be an unsafe time to be a Pagan: build masks and safety zones. Masks made up of the pieces of ourselves and pieces from those who love us, a mask made of truth and of what we want to show the world. So that it takes the hit when people shout Witch and throw things, and it won’t break – or it might break (so you don’t break) and then you can rebuild it.

3. You all know Tower Time is here: the tower falls down, but you can’t rebuild while the rubble is still there. Sometimes you can take it away in a truck, sometimes you need to use dynamite – in the metaphor, it’s a metaphor in life, you are all intelligent and will figure it out.

Odin has spent so much time to find allies so that things will continue after Ragnarök. He has traded everything and He has stolen knowledge. Baldur will step forward out of Helheim and kindle life again.
-----
My reaction is that I find these to be rather trite or at least speaking to the audience. I have no idea about any of them.

My reason for bringing this up is that it seems to have sparked a mania of sorts. People are saying that yes that is what is happening since the Supreme Court did not do what we wanted them to do, etc, and all those nasty Christians are trying to destroy us, etc. And yes, Loki's words give us direction.
----
1. Does this particular mania indicate that the current forms of Neo-Paganism are on the wane?
2. Does this mania become harmful or simply burns itself out?
3. Besides divination and meditation, how do you tell the veracity of your own interpretation of this or any message from a God?

As for me, I have had direct experience with two Gods. At first, I couldn't think other than my immediate concerns. After my brain injury, I had a completely different point of view of what They said. So as far as these messages are concerned, I do not pay much attention to them.

Any other thoughts?

replies.title: Re: Loki's messages going around in the Neo-Pagan blogsphere: Mania

Hmm. Loki being Loki, I really wonder why people are assuming that he's saying these things in good faith; he's at least as likely to be messing with them, telling them what they want to hear, in order to trip them up in some way that he'll find hilarious. Trickster gods are like that.

Beyond that -- well, we'll see what happens. I'm pretty sure that Neopaganism is on its way to join Spiritualism and Theosophy in the graveyard of old American religious fads, leaving a few congregations here and there to go through the motions for a while to come. But we'll see.

This came to mind because I was reading some old notes and I came across this version of an Irish pentagram ritual, which someone posted to your Galabes blog ages ago:

***

1. The Rite of the Triquetra

First, stand facing east. Imagine yourself expanding to a vast height until your head is among the stars, the sun is at your solar plexus and you stand upon the round earth. Then raise your right hand above your head, and draw it down to your solar plexus. As you do this, visualize a ray of light descending from infinite space above you to formulate a star of brilliant white light above the top of you head. Say “Iarram ar na cumhachtaí Talam”.

Second, bring your hand it in an arc that takes it past your right nipple and then out to the top of your left shoulder, saying “ar Muir,”

Third, bring it in another arc down past your heart and then up again to the top of your right shoulder, saying “agus Nem.”

Fourth, bring it in a third arc past your left nipple and then back to your solar plexus, then extend both arms out to the sides, angling downward. Say “Beidh sé amlaidh!”

2. The Summoning Ritual of the Pentagram

This is performed as follows:

First, face east and perform the complete Rite of the Triqueta

Second, go to the eastern quarter of the circle. Using the first two fingers of your right hand, with the others folded under your thumb in the palm of your hand, trace a summoning pentagram, starting from the upper point and moving clockwise. The pentagram should be two or three feet across. As you do this, visualize it taking shape before you, as though your fingers were drawing it in a line of golden light. When you have finished, point to the center and vibrate the divine name Lugh

Third, with your extended fingers, trace a line around the circumference of the circle a quarter circle to your right, ending at the southern quarter of the circle; visualize it in golden light as you trace it. Trace a pentagram in the south in the same way as the east, point to the center and vibrate the divine name Brig.

Fourth, repeat the process, tracing a line another quarter of the way around the circle to the west, visualizing it in golden light, and tracing and visualizing the pentagram as you did in the east and south. Point to the center of the pentagram and vibrate the divine name Ogma.

Fifth, repeat the process once more, tracing a line around to the north, tracing and visualizing a pentagram there, and vibrating the divine name Danu.

Sixth, trace a line as before back around to the east, completing the circle, and then return to the center and face east. Extend your arms up and out to your sides, taking on the posture of the Triquetra. Say:

Before me the Sword of Finias and the powers of air. Behind me the Cauldron of Murias and the powers of water. To my right hand the Spear of Gorias and the powers of fire.
To my left hand the Stone of Falias and the powers of earth. For about me flame the pentagrams, and upon me shine the Triquetra of Light.

As you name each of these things, visualize it as solidly and intensely as you can. You are surrounded by the elements – a cloudscape to the east, a desert scene of blazing heat and flame to the south, a seascape to the west, and a forest to the north – with the divine artifacts visible against these backgrounds. The pentagrams and circle form a pattern like a crown surrounding you, and the Triquetra of Light shine down from the starry center of light in your head as in the Rite of the Triquetra.

Seventh, perform the complete Rite of the Triquetra as before. This completes the ritual.

***

Reading through the ritual, I can feel a strong energy from it, which leads me to wonder if someone's been out there working with it-- or if it's time for someone to start.

replies.title: Re: Incarnation Variations
replies.content: Various kinds of nonhuman beings can be incarnated in human bodies. Dion Fortune has an essay in her book Applied Magic titled "Non-Humans" which discusses this in some detail. It can happen with elemental spirits, elemental intelligences, beings of the sort that folklore calls "faeries," and demonic entities; every so often, it happens with gods -- thus the accounts of avatars and divine incarnations found in so many traditions. replies.title: Re: color scales QBL
replies.content: The color scales were a creation of Moina Mathers, one of the leading figures in the original Golden Dawn; she was an artist and a clairvoyant, and worked out the color scales as part of her personal magical work. comments.title: Q
comments.content:
Sir,

I had a question on idolatry and what are we worship when we worship gods/spirits as statues/or postcards or idols? Is our worship an astral phenomena for whom the physical object is a pointer to?

What are your thoughts on what abrahamic religions have to say about his vs a more gnostic tradition?

replies.title: Re: Q
replies.content:
1) A statue of a deity is a symbol, nothing more or less. It's a way of helping us concentrate our intentions on the deity we're invoking. It shouldn't stop on the astral level -- worship should always aim for the spiritual planes, which are beyond form.

2) The Abrahamic faiths practice idolatry toward their sacred books. They prefer verbal idols to visual ones -- which is fine, but doesn't justify the nastiness with which their adherents too often like to treat those who make the other choice.

replies.title: Re: Q

Sir,

replies.title: Re: Q
replies.content: Pray with intention and ask the deity to help you get past the astral. The spiritual planes are deities' home turf, and they're the ones who can help us experience those.

replies.title: Re: Q
replies.content: Depends on the Abrahamic faith. In Orthodoxy, 1) is similar to how we view icons (checks over shoulder, bracing for protestants to pounce, shrieking "second commandment violation!"). They're not gods. They're paint and wood, but also windows, or points of connection, to Christ and to the saints depicted.  We have some rules about that-- only persons who were physically incarnated can be directly portrayed in icons. The Trinity, the Holy Spirit, and God the Father, cannot be portrayed directly. So that whole Sistine Chapel creation scene.... that would be verboten in an Orthodox church. We can only represent those things indirectly, which is why, eg, Rublev's famous icon of the trinity doesn't portray the trinity, but rather the three angels who visited Abraham (the icon is also called the hospitality of Abraham), who were sort of a foreshadowing of it. In the story, they are physically present, so they're permissible.

replies.title: Re: Q
replies.content: Thanks for this. What are the Orthodox customs relating to the Bible?

replies.title: Re: Q
replies.content:
Short version: We edited and assembled the Bible, for our own use.

Long version: The church existed before the Bible. In the beginning, there was the Septuagint (the Greek translation of Hebrew scripture favored by early Christians), there were some gospels (firsthand accounts by people who knew Jesus), and there were a bunch of letters and stuff written by the apostles and early church fathers to the various churches, about various subjects. Lots and lots of writings in circulation. Some of them kind of dubious. Lots of them hotly debated. So about three hundred-odd years in, there was a council about various controversies in the church, and at that council, the official canon was finalized. A bunch of stuff didn't make the cut-- and not all of it because it was considered heretical. Some of it wasn't totally essential, some had questionable provenance-- various reasons. Protoevangelion of James didn't make it, but it's where we get most of church tradition about the life of the Theotokos. It's not dogma, but everyone believes it anyway ;)

So, yeah. We're pretty sure that God had a hand in the choosing, the compilation has worked out pretty well for us, but... the Bible is a collection of sacred texts that the council of Constantinople (?) put its official seal of approval on, to say "look, these are official. We can't vouch for the rest, but these are the ones you can use liturgically (except Revelations-- that's never read liturgically), they're important, and useful for the salvation of our souls. So use them." That's it. The Bible is essential, we study it, we meditate on its words, but it's not the... (useless hand-flailing). It exists in a context. That context is the church that compiled it, for the use of the church. Does that make sense? It's not holy in and of itself. Only God is holy. We venerate the Gospel separately, much as we venerate icons, relics, and other sacred things. Not the whole Bible, though.

Orthodox are pretty miffed by the protestant idea that you can derive authority from the Bible, AND ALSO that it's totally OK for someone like Luther to come along and start lopping off books he doesn't like, willy-nilly (and they all use this amputated version!). I mean, you derive your authority from this text, but you also get to *edit* it?? And what about the "early church" so many of them go on about-- the theoretically pure, uncorrupted pre-Catholic Christians they think they're emulating? They didn't have the Bible yet. Where did they get their authority? It's very confusing.

So... the Bible. We put that together, and we think it's so important, and so useful for salvation, that we read it and quote it repeatedly in every church service. We like it a lot. It's one of many tools in the Orthodox toolkit for getting closer to God. If you get too monomaniacally obsessed with it, your confessor might tell you to leave it on the shelf and use some of the other tools for a change.

The dream was also scary enough to make me say the last line of the SOP (may the holy powers bless and protect me), which brings me to my second question: if you're doing the SOP daily, is it enough to visualize the sphere, or do you need to go through all seven invocations again?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) There's not necessarily a difference between these two things. Dreams are one of the ways we interact with the astral plane.

2) Do the whole thing. The sphere alone doesn't have that much clout.

comments.title: Bandit saints of Java by George Quinn
comments.content:
Hey jmg and others

I have recently finished a rather amazing book about religion in Java and Indonesia in general called “Bandit saints of Java” by George Quinn.

It describes a lot of interesting facts about the religious and mystical beliefs of the Javanese people, particularly how the newer Islam and the older Hindu-Buddhist mysticism of Java have interacted and still interact.

The bandit-saint of the books title was a Gautama-like figure who after learning about the suffering of the world outside his fathers palace runs away to become a “Robin Hood” before meeting with a older Muslim saint who gives him a magic staff to guard and meditate upon. Many years later the saint finds him in the same spot surrounded by jungle, still meditating. He wakes him up and takes him under his wing.

replies.title: Re: Bandit saints of Java by George Quinn
replies.content: Interesting. I'll consider it.

replies.title: Re: Bandit saints of Java by George Quinn
replies.content:
It is definitely worth a look, as it does have some things in it that touch on the themes that you have written about, such as spirits that don’t fit standard categories ,such as the tuyul, deformed baby like spirits that bring wealth to the owner so long as they can tolerate it’s childish tantrums and demands.
It also mentions conflict between the popular folk-Islam called abangan and orthodox Islam called Santri which parallels what you write about mass media erasing local cultures and replacing it with one culture.
It also mentions a kind of native millenialism centring around the return of “the righteous king” ratu adal, a Chinese variety of financial or horary astrology, and a pagan spirit that successfully chides a Muslim saint for punishing unbelievers.
